title: Genesis 47
subtitle: Jacob Before Pharaoh; Joseph's Land Policy; Jacob's Final Years

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Joseph Rules Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — Jacob Before Pharaoh; Settlement in Goshen

poi.name: Goshen
poi.text: Jacob presents the Goshen settlement to Pharaoh as a request and Pharaoh confirms it. Goshen is explicitly "the best part of the land." The covenant community's Egyptian geography is the most fertile zone — an irony that will become the motive for later oppression.

poi.name: Egypt under Joseph's administration
poi.text: Joseph's famine management reorganizes Egyptian land ownership: people trade livestock, then land, then themselves for grain. Only the priests' land is exempt. Joseph creates the economic structure that Pharaoh's later oppression of Israel will exploit.

notes.note: MacArthur: Jacob blessed Pharaoh -- the covenant patriarch blesses the most powerful ruler on earth. The blessing follows the Abrahamic pattern: the covenant community is to be a blessing to the nations (12:3). Jacob's 'few and difficult' years (v.9) are the covenant man's honest assessment of a life that was simultaneously filled with promise and pain.

MacArthur: Joseph settled his family in the best part of Egypt (Rameses) and provided for them according to the number of children. The providential reversal is complete: the son sold into slavery now provides for the family that sold him. Joseph's care for his father and brothers is covenant faithfulness expressed in practical provision.

heb.gloss: to declare / to present / to tell
heb.paragraph: Joseph "went and told Pharaoh (wayyaggēd lĕparʿōh), 'My father and brothers... have come'" (v.1). The verb nāgad ("to declare clearly") is the standard verb of formal report — used throughout the Joseph narrative for authoritative communication. Joseph's careful orchestration of his family's presentation to Pharaoh reflects his administrative skill: he prepares Pharaoh before the encounter, coaches his brothers on what to say (46:33-34), and presents them as shepherds to secure Goshen. The covenant family's settlement in Goshen is secured through diplomatic skill, not merely divine miracle.

heb.gloss: let them, I pray / please let them
heb.paragraph: Jacob's request — "please let your servants settle in Goshen" (v.4) — uses the particle nāʾ (please/I pray), which softens the request with deference. The geographical result — Goshen, the best land for shepherds — is both practically advantageous (separation from Egyptian society that despised shepherds, v.34) and theologically significant: the covenant family lives in a distinct territory within Egypt, maintaining their identity as a separate people rather than assimilating. The Goshen settlement is preparation for the Exodus — a people who remain distinct can be led out as a distinct nation.

"Jacob blessed Pharaoh" (v.7, 10) — the patriarch blesses the most powerful man in the world. The repeated blessing-action (Jacob blesses Pharaoh twice — before and after the interview) enacts the Abrahamic covenant: "all peoples on earth will be blessed through you" (12:3). The sojourner blesses the sovereign. The ger blessing the melek. The covenant promise of universal blessing operates through the covenant patriarch even in the moment of family dependence and political vulnerability.

hist.historical: Jacob's audience before Pharaoh is the most formal scene of the patriarchal narrative. An elderly Canaanite shepherd stands before the ruler of the ancient world and blesses him — twice. The blessing of the lesser by the greater is expected; but here the greater is blessed by the lesser, which the author of Hebrews will note (Heb 7:7). Jacob's answer to Pharaoh's question about his age — "my years have been few and difficult" — is the lament of a man who has known more grief than joy, spoken at the moment of his greatest earthly consolation.
hist.context: Jacob blessing Pharaoh (vv.7,10) is a remarkable reversal of power dynamics: the nomadic patriarch blesses the most powerful ruler on earth twice. The text twice notes it (vv.7,10), underscoring its significance. This is the Abrahamic promise in action — "in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" includes the family of Pharaoh. Jacob's burial request (vv.29–31) is the chapter's final theological statement: Egypt is not home; Canaan is. The covenant land must receive the patriarch's bones.

refs.note: "In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" — Jacob blesses Pharaoh.
Jacob's burial request is fulfilled — Joseph takes him back to Canaan.
The patriarchs "acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" — Jacob's language of "sojourning" in Pharaoh's court is the NT's paradigm of faith-pilgrim identity.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The double blessing of Pharaoh by Jacob is highlighted by Sarna as the fulfilment of the Abrahamic covenant's promise that through Abraham's descendants all peoples would be blessed (12:3). Sarna: "The patriarch who has been the passive recipient of God's covenant promises now becomes the active agent of blessing to the nations. The covenant is not merely received — it is transmitted, even to the most powerful rulers of the earth."

Sarna on Jacob's self-description: "'Few and difficult' — the Hebrew me'at vera'im captures Jacob's honest self-assessment. The 'few' is comparative: his life has been shorter than his forebears. The 'difficult' is experiential: the content of his years has been harder. Jacob is not complaining before Pharaoh; he is answering a direct question with characteristic honesty. The same man who was capable of deception is also capable of this kind of painful candour."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jacob's answer to Pharaoh is one of the most memorable speeches in Genesis. Alter: "The response has the quality of a lament spoken at the wrong moment — a moment of maximal earthly consolation (Joseph found, family in Egypt, Pharaoh's favour). Yet Jacob names his life 'few and difficult.' This is not ingratitude but accuracy: Jacob's life has been defined by grief, deception, and loss. The consolation of the final years does not erase the pain of the preceding ones."

The double blessing frames the encounter. Alter notes the structural symmetry: Jacob blesses Pharaoh when he enters (v.7) and again when he departs (v.10). The encounter is bracketed by blessing. The patriarch's covenant function — to be a blessing — is enacted in the most public and politically significant arena of his life.

Calvin on Jacob's self-description: "Jacob does not say that his life has been wretched — only that it has been difficult. He knows that God has been faithful; he also knows that the content of his years has been marked by suffering. Both things are true. The man of faith does not pretend that suffering does not exist; he carries it honestly while still holding to the covenant."

Calvin on the blessing of Pharaoh: "Jacob, though he was a stranger and inferior in earthly standing, blessed Pharaoh — not as a mere social courtesy but as the covenant patriarch who carried the blessing of God for the nations. The greater, in terms of earthly power, received blessing from the lesser in earthly terms but the greater in terms of covenant standing."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "few and difficult" discusses the Hebrew me'at (few, small) and ra'im (difficult, evil, hard). The note observes that Jacob is 130 — not an especially short life — yet he calls it "few" in comparison to his fathers (Abraham: 175; Isaac: 180). The note: "Jacob's statement is not false modesty; it is a reflection of his experiential sense of his life's quality, not merely its length."

The NET note on Jacob blessing Pharaoh cross-references Hebrews 7:7 ("without doubt the lesser is blessed by the greater") and observes that the author of Hebrews uses this scene to illustrate the superiority of the Melchizedekian priesthood. The covenant blessing carried by Jacob is, in this encounter, treated as superior to the political power carried by Pharaoh.

tl.name: Jacob enters Egypt (year 2) — this passage
tl.text: Five famine years remain. Joseph's administration trades grain for livestock, then land, then personal freedom — reorganizing Egyptian society. The covenant family settles in Goshen while Egypt is restructured around them.

notes.note: MacArthur: The Egyptians sold themselves and their land to Pharaoh in exchange for food during the famine -- an ironic counterpoint to Joseph's earlier enslavement. Joseph, the Hebrew slave, now administers the system by which Egyptians become bound to Pharaoh. The reversal of circumstances is the framework within which God's providence has operated.

MacArthur: 'You have saved our lives; may we find favour in the eyes of our lord' -- the Egyptians' gratitude to Joseph is the narrative's comment on the covenant community's role in the world. Joseph's wisdom, given by God, saved Egypt. The covenant man's gifts are for the blessing of the nations, not only his own people.

heb.paragraph: "There was no food (leḥem) in the whole region because the famine was severe" (v.13) — the leḥem crisis drives the population of Egypt and Canaan into increasing dependence on Joseph's administration. The narrative traces the progressive stages: money (v.14), livestock (v.16-17), land (v.20), and bodies (v.21). The word leḥem (bread/food) links back to the Bethlehem narrative (leḥem = bet-leḥem, "house of bread") and forward to the Manna: the Creator gives or withholds food according to his purposes, and human survival depends on what he provides.

Joseph's taxation system — "a fifth of the produce belongs to Pharaoh" (v.26) — implements the covenant-preparation principle. Twenty percent tax to Pharaoh means the people retain eighty percent, which is generous compared to other ancient taxation systems. The narrator notes: "only the land of the priests did not become Pharaoh's" (v.22, 26). The priestly exemption foreshadows the Levitical exemptions in Israel's law. Joseph's economic administration is a proto-covenant economic structure — taxation with priestly exemption, embedded within a pagan state.

"You have saved our lives (hĕḥayîtānu)" (v.25) — the Egyptians' response to Joseph's grain-for-land policy. The verb ḥāyāh ("to live/save life") connects Joseph's economic rescue to the deeper theological theme: Joseph is the one who gives life (ḥayyîm) in the midst of death (māwet). His brothers' future statement — "we are your slaves (ʿabādîm)" (50:18) — uses the same submission language. Joseph, the one sold as an ʿebed (slave), becomes the one who receives ʿabādîm. The reversal is the narrative's theology of divine vindication.

hist.historical: Joseph's famine administration is the most economically detailed narrative in Genesis. Over the course of the seven-year famine, Joseph purchases all of Egypt's money, livestock, land, and ultimately the persons of the Egyptians for Pharaoh — creating a feudal system in which the crown owns all the land, the people are Pharaoh's tenants, and one-fifth of all production is paid as tax. Only the temple lands (priestly estates) are exempt. The policy has been debated for centuries: is it exploitation or salvation?
hist.context: Jacob blessing Pharaoh (vv.7,10) is a remarkable reversal of power dynamics: the nomadic patriarch blesses the most powerful ruler on earth twice. The text twice notes it (vv.7,10), underscoring its significance. This is the Abrahamic promise in action — "in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" includes the family of Pharaoh. Jacob's burial request (vv.29–31) is the chapter's final theological statement: Egypt is not home; Canaan is. The covenant land must receive the patriarch's bones.

refs.note: "In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" — Jacob blesses Pharaoh.
Jacob's burial request is fulfilled — Joseph takes him back to Canaan.
The patriarchs "acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" — Jacob's language of "sojourning" in Pharaoh's court is the NT's paradigm of faith-pilgrim identity.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The acquisition of Egypt's land and people by Pharaoh through Joseph's administration is historically attested. Sarna: "Egyptian records from the Second Intermediate Period and New Kingdom confirm that a significant proportion of Egyptian land was owned by the crown and the temples, with the population working as tenant farmers. The biblical narrative's description of Joseph's policy corresponds to the land-tenure system that characterised ancient Egypt."

The exemption of the priestly estates from Joseph's land purchase is a significant detail. Sarna: "The priests received regular rations from Pharaoh and did not need to sell their land. The exemption confirms Joseph's political sophistication: he did not antagonise the priestly establishment, which was the most powerful non-royal institution in Egypt."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The economic transformation sequence — money, then livestock, then land, then persons — has a relentless economic logic. Alter: "Each stage follows from the previous with the logic of a famine in which survival trumps ownership. The people who accepted the transaction understood what they were doing: 'you have saved our lives' (v.25). Joseph is not exploiting the vulnerable — he is providing survival at the cost of permanent economic restructuring."

The statute "one-fifth to Pharaoh" as "a law in Egypt to this day" is the narrative's acknowledgment that Joseph's policy had lasting historical consequences. Alter: "The narrator is recording a real historical institution and attributing its origin to Joseph. The famine administration is not merely a story detail but a claim about the origins of Egyptian land tenure."

Calvin on the land purchase: "Joseph conducted himself wisely and uprightly in this business. He did not enrich Pharaoh by rapine or extortion; the people themselves freely offered their land for food. He made a fair exchange. The system he created was a feudal arrangement that the people accepted voluntarily as the price of their lives."

Calvin on the lasting statute: "This shows that Joseph acted with political wisdom — he established the arrangement as a law, not merely an emergency measure. The institutionalisation of the tithing arrangement gave it permanence and prevented future arbitrariness. Joseph thought beyond the immediate crisis to the long-term ordering of Egyptian society."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "he reduced the people to servitude" discusses the two main textual traditions: the MT has "he moved them to cities" (he'evir otam la'arim); the LXX and Samaritan Pentateuch have "he made them slaves" (la'avadim). The note: "The LXX reading corresponds better to the context (people offering themselves as slaves, v.19) and may reflect the original text."

The NET note on the twenty percent tax (one-fifth) observes this is consistent with ancient Near Eastern tithing rates for royal estates. The note: "A twenty-percent agricultural tax — to be paid from every harvest — was within the range of ancient land-tenure systems, neither unusually exploitative nor unusually generous. The people's response ('you have saved our lives') suggests they regarded it as reasonable."

sections.header: Verses 27–31 — Israel Prospers; Jacob's Final Request

notes.note: MacArthur: Jacob's deathbed oath -- 'do not bury me in Egypt' -- is the final act of covenant faith. He insists on burial in Canaan when Canaan is not possessed, holding the promise as real even in death. The writer of Hebrews (11:21) includes Jacob's worship at the head of his bed (v.31) among the great acts of faith, alongside Abraham's Akedah.

MacArthur: Jacob's deathbed request to be buried in Canaan reflects the same faith exhibited throughout his life's second half. The promise of Canaan was not yet fulfilled in any material sense, but Jacob treats it as certain. The patriarch's final act is covenant confidence expressed through burial instructions.

heb.paragraph: Israel "lived (wayyēšeb) in Egypt in the region of Goshen" (v.27) — but the verb shifts in the next clause: "they acquired property there and were fruitful and increased greatly in number." The settlement that looks like permanent dwelling (yāšab) is actually the Abrahamic covenant operating in exile: "be fruitful and multiply" (1:28; 9:1) is being fulfilled in Goshen even while the covenant land awaits. The fruitfulness in Egypt is the preparation for the people whose Exodus will fulfil the land promise.

Jacob's deathbed request — "Put your hand under my thigh and promise (ḥesed weʾĕmet) that you will not bury me in Egypt" (v.29) — is the thigh-oath already used by Abraham (24:2), connecting Jacob's request to the patriarchal covenant framework. The specific requirement — burial in "my fathers' burial place" (v.30, i.e., Machpelah, purchased in ch.23) — insists that Jacob's body returns to the covenant land. His physical interment in Canaan is the covenant declaration that the exile is temporary and the land promise remains in force.

"Israel bowed in worship (wayyištaḥû yiśrāʾēl) as he leaned on the top of his staff" (v.31) — the verb šāḥāh (to bow down/worship) appears at this moment of covenant commitment and death-approach. Jacob-Israel bows not to Joseph but in worship of God — a posture of covenant faith at the threshold of death. The staff he leans on connects to the shepherd-pilgrim imagery of the entire patriarchal narrative: he has been a sojourner his whole life (47:9: "my years have been... few and difficult") and now he worships as he approaches the end.

hist.historical: Israel prospers in Goshen while Jacob approaches death. Jacob's request that Joseph not bury him in Egypt but carry him back to Canaan — the oath sworn with hand under the thigh — is both a personal wish and a theological statement: Jacob belongs to the land of promise, not to Egypt. The oath is the most solemn form available. "Do not bury me in Egypt" is the dying patriarch's confession that Egypt is not home, however prosperous.
hist.context: Jacob blessing Pharaoh (vv.7,10) is a remarkable reversal of power dynamics: the nomadic patriarch blesses the most powerful ruler on earth twice. The text twice notes it (vv.7,10), underscoring its significance. This is the Abrahamic promise in action — "in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" includes the family of Pharaoh. Jacob's burial request (vv.29–31) is the chapter's final theological statement: Egypt is not home; Canaan is. The covenant land must receive the patriarch's bones.

refs.note: "In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" — Jacob blesses Pharaoh.
Jacob's burial request is fulfilled — Joseph takes him back to Canaan.
The patriarchs "acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" — Jacob's language of "sojourning" in Pharaoh's court is the NT's paradigm of faith-pilgrim identity.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The flourishing of Israel in Goshen — "they acquired property, were fruitful, and increased greatly in number" — uses three verbs that echo the creation mandate (1:28) and the Abrahamic promise. Sarna: "The covenant promises are being fulfilled in Egypt, not in Canaan. The geographical displacement that seemed to threaten the covenant is in fact the mechanism of its fulfilment. The great nation promised to Abraham is growing in Egypt."

Jacob worshipping at the head of his bed after receiving Joseph's oath is one of the last gestures of the patriarch. Sarna: "The posture of worship — bowing at the head of the bed — is an act of thanksgiving for the oath received. Jacob, who has been led by God through deception, grief, exile, and reunion, ends with an act of worship. The man who wrestled with God at Peniel now bows in gratitude."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The deathbed oath is the patriarchal tradition's formal mechanism for binding the next generation to the covenant geography. Alter: "Jacob does not merely ask to be buried in Canaan — he requires an oath sworn by the most solemn available means. He knows Joseph is an Egyptian official with an Egyptian life. The oath is necessary to ensure that the commitment outlasts the convenience of the moment."

Jacob's requirement to be buried in Canaan claims the promised land by the act of burial — the same way Abraham claimed it in ch.23. Alter notes both acts treat the promise as already real: Canaan is where the covenant people belong, even when history temporarily takes them elsewhere.

Calvin on Jacob's worship after the oath: "He worshipped at the head of his bed — the holy man, already in the weakness of approaching death, gathered his strength for an act of gratitude. He had waited for this assurance his whole life. Now it is given: he will rest with his fathers. His faith triumphs over his weakness."

Calvin: Jacob sealed the oath with worship, giving thanks that he had been preserved so long and would now at last rest in the promised land. The posture of worship at the deathbed is the ultimate testimony of faith.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "Israel bowed down at the head of his bed" discusses the LXX's translation "he worshipped, leaning on the top of his staff" (rabb'a as staff rather than mittah as bed). Hebrews 11:21 follows the LXX. The note: "The Hebrew mateh can mean either bed or staff — the difference depends on the vowel pointing, which was added later. Both traditions have good support. The theological point — Jacob's act of worship — is not affected."

The NET note on the "hand under the thigh" oath explains it as the most solemn form of personal oath in ancient Israelite tradition — used in Genesis only for commitments of ultimate importance (24:2; 47:29). The note suggests the thigh represents the seat of procreation and family continuity; the oath binds the swearer to the sworn obligation as a covenant of family identity.

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: The patriarch's two great acts in this chapter: blessing the most powerful man on earth, and insisting on burial in Canaan — covenant identity maintained to the end.

ppl.name: Joseph
ppl.text: His grain policy (vv.13–26) saves Egypt but raises ethical questions about the concentration of power. His management is effective; its long-term consequences are complex.

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: Blessed by the Semitic patriarch twice — the Abrahamic promise operating at the highest levels of human power.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"The days of the years of my sojourning are 130 years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life."NIV"The years of my pilgrimage are a hundred and thirty. My years have been few and difficult."KJV"The days of the years of my pilgrimage are an hundred and thirty years: few and evil have the days of the years of my life been."

src.title: Egyptian Famine Administration
src.quote: Administrative papyri document the centralisation of grain distribution in times of famine. Joseph's policy (vv.13–26) — acquiring land for Pharaoh in exchange for grain — is consistent with known Egyptian administrative practice.
src.note: The economic details of ch.47 are culturally plausible.

src.title: Burial Customs
src.quote: Repatriation of bones to ancestral land is attested in the ancient Near East. The patriarchal insistence on Canaan burial reflects covenant-territorial identity.
src.note: Jacob's burial request is culturally intelligible within ANE practice.

rec.text: The epistle uses the patriarchal "sojourning" language to define Christian identity: "strangers and exiles on the earth." Jacob's self-description before Pharaoh becomes the NT paradigm for all covenant people.

Jacob blessing Pharaoh twice is for Calvin the demonstration that covenant blessing flows outward — the weakest (in human terms) blesses the strongest, because the covenant blessing belongs to the one who carries it, not to the one with the most power.

rows.label: Jacob Blesses Pharaoh
rows.text: Jacob blesses Pharaoh twice -- when he enters and when he leaves. The lesser blesses the greater in the kingdom's economy. The pilgrim patriarch whose years have been few and difficult occupies the position of blessing-giver.

rows.label: The Deathbed Covenant Oath
rows.text: Do not bury me in Egypt. The deathbed oath is Jacob's final act of covenant faith: insisting on burial in Canaan when Canaan is not possessed, treating the promise as already real.

tx.title: Moving the people to cities — a major textual variant
tx.content: MT reads 'he moved the people to the cities' (heʿĕbîr ʾōtō leʿārîm) — Joseph relocated Egypt's population. The LXX reads 'he made them slaves' (κατεδουλώσατο αὐτούς), a harsher description. The SP reads 'he moved them to cities,' agreeing with MT. This is one of the more significant narrative variants in late Genesis.
tx.note: The LXX's 'enslaved' may reflect awareness of Egyptian corvée labor traditions. Both readings yield a portrait of total state control, but the LXX's is considerably darker.

tx.title: Bed or staff? — MT vs LXX
tx.content: MT reads 'Israel bowed in worship at the head of the bed' (hammiṭṭāh). The LXX reads 'on the top of his staff' (τῆς ῥάβδου), reading maṭṭeh (staff) instead of miṭṭāh (bed). Hebrews 11:21 follows the LXX: 'by faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of Joseph's sons, leaning on the top of his staff.'
tx.note: This is one of the most famous MT/LXX variants in Genesis. The consonantal text (mṭh) is identical — only the vowels differ. Hebrews' use of the LXX reading gives it canonical authority for Christians.

debate.title: Joseph's Famine Policy: Economic Wisdom or Oppression?

positions.name: Oppression / exploitation
positions.argument: Joseph systematically strips the Egyptian population of money, livestock, land, and personal freedom in exchange for food during a crisis. This is a forced transfer of wealth from the people to the crown, exploiting the famine.

positions.name: Salvation and reasonable reorganisation
positions.argument: The people voluntarily make the exchanges; they explicitly say "you have saved our lives" (v.25) and accept the 20% tax as fair. The policy created a stable agricultural economy that outlasted the crisis. No one died of famine in Egypt under Joseph's administration.
